summary: I was born and raised in Vietnam, where I spent my early years surrounded by my family and friends. Life there was simple but meaningful, especially in my hometown where my parents worked making tofu and soy milk to sell. I studied up to 9th grade in Vietnam before deciding to leave for better opportunities. Although I moved away, I still deeply miss my friends and the familiar places back home.

I was born in Ho Chi Minh City, one of the biggest and most vibrant cities in Vietnam. It’s a bustling place full of laughter and life, where I grew up with my family in the Thu Duc district. This city shaped much of my childhood and memories before I left for the United States. Even now, I often think back to the energy and beauty of Ho Chi Minh City.

My family and I lived in Thu Duc, a peaceful district within Ho Chi Minh City. It was a calm and quiet place compared to the busy city center, which made it a comfortable home for us. My parents ran their tofu and soy milk business there, and I spent my childhood in this neighborhood. Leaving Thu Duc was hard because it was where all my close friends and neighbors were.

I attended Tam Phu High School in Vietnam, which was also called the 'Green School' because it was surrounded by many trees and everyone cared about protecting the environment. It was during my time there that I became addicted to video games, often spending whole days playing and even neglecting school. This school was an important part of my early education before I graduated 9th grade and moved to the United States.

I came to the United States after graduating 9th grade in Vietnam because I wanted better educational opportunities and a chance to start fresh. Arriving here changed my life completely; I met new people, made friends, and developed positive habits. I came with my family and brought with me pictures of my loved ones to remind me of home. The United States gave me a second chance to work hard and build a proud future.

I arrived in Minnesota in September 2016, which marked the beginning of a new chapter in my life. Living in Minnesota introduced me to new experiences and people, and it felt like a fresh start. Although I left behind my bike, neighbors, and friends in Vietnam, Minnesota became the place where I could pursue my dreams and study hard. This state holds a special place in my journey toward building a better future.
